title: दक्षिणामूर्ति पञ्चरत्नम्
devanagariText:
॥ श्री दक्षिणामूर्ति पञ्चरत्नम् ॥

विश्वं दर्पणदृश्यमाननगरीतुल्यं निजान्तर्गतम्
पश्यन्नात्मनि मायया बहिरिवोद्भूतं यथा निद्रया।
यः साक्षात्कुरुते प्रबोधसमये स्वात्मानमेवाद्वयम्
तस्मै श्रीगुरुमूर्तये नम इदं श्रीदक्षिणामूर्तये॥ १॥

बीजस्यान्तरिवाङ्कुरो जगदिदं प्राङ्निर्विकल्पं पुनः
मायाकल्पितदेशकालकलनावैचित्र्यचित्रीकृतम्।
मायावीव विजृम्भयत्यपि महायोगीव यः स्वेच्छया
तस्मै श्रीगुरुमूर्तये नम इदं श्रीदक्षिणामूर्तये॥ २॥

यस्यैव स्फुरणं सदात्मकमसत्कल्पार्थकं भासते
साक्षात्तत्त्वमसीति वेदवचसा यो बोधयत्याश्रितान्।
यत्साक्षात्करणाद्भवेन्न पुनरावृत्तिर्भवाम्भोनिधौ
तस्मै श्रीगुरुमूर्तये नम इदं श्रीदक्षिणामूर्तये॥ ३॥

नानाच्छिद्रघटोदरस्थितमहादीपप्रभाभास्वरम्
ज्ञानं यस्य तु चक्षुरादिकरणद्वारा बहिः स्पन्दते।
जानामीति तमेव भान्तमनुभात्येतत्समस्तं जगत्
तस्मै श्रीगुरुमूर्तये नम इदं श्रीदक्षिणामूर्तये॥ ४॥

देहं प्राणमपीन्द्रियाण्यपि चलां बुद्धिं च शून्यं विदुः
स्त्रीबालान्धजडोपमास्त्वहमिति भ्रान्ता भृशं वादिनः।
मायाशक्तिविलासकल्पितमहाव्यामोहसंहारिणे
तस्मै श्रीगुरुमूर्तये नम इदं श्रीदक्षिणामूर्तये॥ ५॥

॥ इति श्री दक्षिणामूर्ति पञ्चरत्नम् सम्पूर्णम् ॥
benefits: Sacred Dakshinamurthy Pancharatnam for devotion and spiritual growth
Invokes blessings of Dakshinamurthy in all forms
Traditionally recited for removal of sins and bestows spiritual merit
Ideal for Dakshinamurthy festivals and daily worship
description: Dakshinamurthy Pancharatnam (दक्षिणामूर्ति पञ्चरत्नम्) is five gems of devotional verses dedicated to Lord Shiva. This revered text originates from Adi Shankaracharya. Devotees recite this prayer to invoke blessings of Dakshinamurthy in all forms and grant divine protection and auspiciousness. It is also said to remove sins and bestow spiritual merit. For best results, devotees chant this on Thursday (Guruvar) and Monday (Somvar) and during Guru Purnima and Maha Shivaratri. The text contains 5 verses and takes around 4 minutes to recite, fitting conveniently into a daily spiritual routine. On this page, you can read the complete Dakshinamurthy Pancharatnam in Devanagari Sanskrit with English transliteration, Hindi meaning (arth), and free PDF download for offline recitation.

seoFaqs.question: What is the Dakshinamurthy Pancharatnam and who composed it?
seoFaqs.answer: The Dakshinamurthy Pancharatnam (panch = five, ratnam = gems) is a five-verse Sanskrit hymn attributed by tradition to Adi Shankaracharya (8th century CE), composed in praise of Lord Dakshinamurti — the south-facing form of Shiva who is the Adi Guru (primordial teacher) of Advaita Vedanta. Each verse is a self-contained teaching on the nature of the Self, taught by the silent guru beneath the banyan tree. The Pancharatnam complements the longer and more famous Dakshinamurthy Stotram (also by Shankara), which begins with the celebrated verse Vishvam darpana drishyamana nagari tulyam. Both are core texts of Smarta Vedantic devotion and are recited especially on Guru Purnima. (Sources: Adi Shankaracharya granthavali; Hindupedia Dakshinamurti; Smarta liturgy.)

seoFaqs.question: Who is Dakshinamurti and why is he the Adi Guru?
seoFaqs.answer: Dakshinamurti is the youthful south-facing form of Shiva (dakshina = south, murti = form), depicted seated beneath a banyan tree in the chin-mudra (the gesture of consciousness, with thumb and index finger touching to form a circle), surrounded by elderly rishis — the four Kumaras (Sanaka, Sanandana, Sanatana and Sanatkumara) — to whom he teaches the highest non-dual wisdom in absolute silence (mauna-vyakhya). This silent transmission, in which the disciples instantly realised Brahman without a single word, is the prototype of all guru-disciple relationships in Advaita Vedanta and the reason Dakshinamurti is hailed as the Adi Guru of all knowledge. The south direction symbolises the direction of death — Dakshinamurti faces it to teach that knowledge alone conquers death. (Sources: Skanda Purana; Adi Shankaracharya bhashyas; Hindupedia Dakshinamurti.)

seoFaqs.question: What is the silent teaching (mauna-vyakhya) of Dakshinamurti?
seoFaqs.answer: The silent teaching (mauna-vyakhya) is the central pedagogical mystery of Dakshinamurti — the Adi Guru transmits the highest wisdom of Brahma-vidya not through speech but through pregnant silence, while seated in the chin-mudra. The four Kumara rishis, despite being aged sages and well-versed in scripture, had failed to attain Brahman through words; sitting before youthful silent Dakshinamurti, they realised the non-dual Self instantly. The teaching is that the ultimate reality cannot be conveyed through language since it is the very witness of language and thought, and only direct experiential transmission from a realised guru in stillness reveals it. The Dakshinamurthy Pancharatnam encodes this teaching in five verses meant for contemplation rather than analysis. (Sources: Adi Shankaracharya bhashya; Hindupedia mauna-vyakhya; Smarta Vedanta tradition.)

seoFaqs.question: When should the Dakshinamurthy Pancharatnam be recited?
seoFaqs.answer: The most powerful occasions are Guru Purnima (Ashadha Purnima, July full moon, the day of all gurus), Maha-Shivaratri, every Thursday (Guruvar — day of the guru), the entire Margashirsha month, the Dakshinayana solstice (when the sun begins its southward journey, the direction Dakshinamurti faces), and at the start of any new study, examination or spiritual practice. Daily recitation in brahma-muhurta (4-6 AM), facing south, before a Dakshinamurti image, with offerings of bilva leaves, white flowers and a single ghee deepa, is the prescribed Smarta sadhana. Students are particularly enjoined to recite this hymn before examinations and at the beginning of each academic year. (Sources: Drikpanchang Guru Purnima; Smarta sadhana texts; Hindupedia Dakshinamurti puja.)

seoFaqs.question: What is the iconography and symbolism of Dakshinamurti?
seoFaqs.answer: Dakshinamurti is depicted as a youthful Shiva with serene face, jata-mukuta (matted hair crown), seated in virasana (hero posture) on a tiger-skin beneath a banyan tree (vata-vriksha — symbol of eternity since it has no apparent beginning), facing south. His right hand displays the chin-mudra (consciousness-gesture symbolising the union of jivatman with Paramatman), his lower right rests on a serpent or holds a rosary, his upper left holds the Vedas (or fire), and his lower left rests on his thigh in the gyana-mudra. His right foot crushes the dwarf Apasmara (forgetfulness/ignorance) while his left foot rests on his right thigh. The four Kumara rishis sit before him in rapt silence. (Sources: Shilpa Shastra; Hindupedia Dakshinamurti iconography; Smarta tradition.)

seoFaqs.question: Where are the major Dakshinamurti temples in India?
seoFaqs.answer: Tamil Nadu has the densest concentration of Dakshinamurti shrines, since virtually every Shiva temple in the state contains a Dakshinamurti niche on the south wall of the sanctum (the prescribed Agamic location). Major dedicated Dakshinamurti shrines include the Alangudi Guru Sthala (one of the navagraha temples — Dakshinamurti is the form of Brihaspati / Jupiter / the cosmic guru), the Dakshinamurti shrine within the Tiruvannamalai Arunachaleshwara temple, the Dakshinamurti at Chidambaram, and the major shrines at Madurai, Thiruvarur and Kanchipuram. Sage Ramana Maharshi at Tiruvannamalai was hailed as a living Dakshinamurti who taught primarily through silence. (Sources: Tamil Nadu HRCE temple records; Hindupedia Dakshinamurti temples; Ramana ashram.)

seoFaqs.question: What benefits does the Dakshinamurthy Pancharatnam promise to its reciters?
seoFaqs.answer: Tradition holds that regular recitation grants brahma-vidya (the highest knowledge of the Self), success in academic and competitive examinations, removal of mental confusion and indecision, the cultivation of viveka (discrimination) and vairagya (detachment), removal of obstacles in spiritual sadhana, the grace of the right guru appearing in one's life (sad-guru-prapti), strengthening of memory and concentration for students, healing from speech defects and learning difficulties (Dakshinamurti being the silent teacher), removal of Brihaspati / Guru graha doshas in the natal chart, and ultimately moksha through Self-realisation as taught by the Adi Guru. The hymn is regarded as supremely auspicious for academic households and students preparing for major examinations. (Sources: Smarta sadhana phalashruti; Hindupedia Dakshinamurti upasana; Adi Shankara tradition.)
